summary: Finnish-American modernist architect and furniture designer. Son of architect Eliel Saarinen. Best known for the Gateway Arch in St. Louis, the TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport, and Dulles International Airport. Also designed the MIT Chapel and Kresge Auditorium, the CBS Building ('Black Rock'), the Miller House in Columbus, Indiana (famous for its conversation pit), and the Ingalls Rink at Yale ('the whale'). Designed iconic furniture for Knoll including the Tulip, Womb, and Grasshopper chairs.

works.name: Gateway Arch (1965)
works.description: The most frequently clued Saarinen work. A stainless steel catenary arch in St. Louis, Missouri — the world's tallest arch — built as the Jefferson Expansion Memorial. The arch design inspired his Dulles Airport roof. Wikipedia

works.name: TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (1962)
works.description: A sculptural terminal building at JFK International Airport, now the TWA Hotel. Famous for its organic, wing-like concrete roof. Described by critic Vincent Scully as exemplifying 'paramilitary dandyism.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Giveaway identifier: the TWA Flight Center at JFK Airport (now TWA Hotel)
examples: A flight center designed by this architect is now the TWA Hotel at JFK airport.

clues.clue: Described by critic Vincent Scully as 'paramilitary dandyism' — showing 'dramatic patience' and 'willful forcing'
examples: 'Paramilitary dandyism' describes the 'dramatic patience' and 'willful forcing' of large 1960s buildings, such as this Finnish architect's CBS Building and TWA Flight Center at JFK International.

works.name: MIT Chapel and Kresge Auditorium (1955)
works.description: Two adjacent buildings on the MIT campus. The nondenominational cylindrical brick chapel features a Harry Bertoia sculpture that cascades light from a skylight to a white marble altar. The Kresge Auditorium is shaped like a leaf — a perfect eighth of a sphere. Wikipedia

works.name: Miller House, Columbus, Indiana (1957)
works.description: A landmark modernist residence in Columbus, Indiana, credited with popularizing the 'conversation pit' — a sunken seating area. The house also features a bas relief by Jacques Lipchitz. Columbus, Indiana became famous for its architecture, including several Saarinen buildings. Wikipedia

works.name: CBS Building / 'Black Rock' (1965)
works.description: A skyscraper in New York City. Saarinen set out to design 'the simplest skyscraper in New York City.' Its facade of dark granite piers earned it the nickname 'The Black Rock.' Wikipedia

works.name: Dulles International Airport (1962)
works.description: Saarinen designed an airfoil-like concrete roof for Washington Dulles International Airport. The design incorporates catenary curves, which also inspired his Gateway Arch. Wikipedia

works.name: Ingalls Rink ('Yale Whale') at Yale University (1958)
works.description: An ice hockey rink at Yale University nicknamed 'the whale' for its sloping roof. Previously used as an all-girls dormitory with a drawbridge to keep men out. Wikipedia

works.name: Tulip, Womb, and Grasshopper Chairs (for Knoll)
works.description: Eero Saarinen designed three iconic furniture pieces manufactured by Knoll: the Tulip Chair (pedestal base), the Womb Chair, and the Grasshopper Chair. The Tulip Chair is in the Vitra Design Museum. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Eero Saarinen is identified and key biographical facts. Son of Finnish architect Eliel Saarinen. Collaborated with Charles Eames on the Entenza House (Case Study House). Also designed the General Motors Technical Center in Warren, Michigan; John Deere, IBM, and CBS headquarters; and the Kleinhans Music Hall in Buffalo (with his father). Collaborated with Eliel on the First Christian Church in Columbus, Indiana.

comprehensive_summary:
Eero Saarinen was a Finnish-American modernist architect and furniture designer, son of the Finnish architect Eliel Saarinen. He is most famous in quizbowl for two structures: the Gateway Arch in St. Louis — the world's tallest arch, built as the Jefferson Expansion Memorial using a catenary curve design — and the TWA Flight Center at JFK International Airport, now converted into the TWA Hotel. The TWA terminal was described by critic Vincent Scully as exemplifying 'paramilitary dandyism,' a term also applied to his CBS Building in New York City, nicknamed 'The Black Rock' for its dark granite pier facade, which Saarinen designed to be 'the simplest skyscraper in New York City.'

At MIT, Saarinen designed two adjacent buildings: the nondenominational red-brick chapel, which features a Harry Bertoia sculpture that creates a cascade of light from a skylight to a white marble altar, and the Kresge Auditorium, shaped like a leaf — a perfect eighth of a sphere. At Yale, he designed the Ingalls Rink, nicknamed 'the whale' for its dramatic sloping roof; one building from that project was previously used as an all-girls dormitory with a drawbridge to keep men out. He also designed Washington Dulles International Airport, featuring an airfoil-like concrete roof incorporating catenary curves (linking it to the Gateway Arch concept).

Saarinen's Miller House in Columbus, Indiana is credited with popularizing the 'conversation pit' — a sunken seating area that became a mid-century modern design icon. The house contains a bas relief by sculptor Jacques Lipchitz. Columbus, Indiana became renowned for its modernist architecture through multiple Saarinen commissions; the 2017 film Columbus by Kogonada was set there. Eero collaborated with his father Eliel on several projects, including the General Motors Technical Center in Warren, Michigan and the Kleinhans Music Hall in Buffalo, New York (which contains the Mary Seaton room). Eliel's 1922 runner-up design for the Chicago Tribune Tower competition influenced the American Radiator Building in New York, and Eliel also directed the Cranbrook Academy of Art, which was strongly influenced by the Bauhaus.

As a furniture designer, Saarinen created three iconic chairs for the Knoll company: the Grasshopper, the Womb, and the Tulip — the last of which is housed in the Vitra Design Museum. He also collaborated with Charles Eames on the Entenza House, a Case Study House adjacent to the famous Eames House. Saarinen designed the Vivian Beaumont Theater at Lincoln Center and corporate headquarters for John Deere, IBM, and CBS. His wife (or his father's wife) Lillian Swann Saarinen was a sculptor who created Night at the 1939 World's Fair, depicting the panther Bagheera, and designed a reflecting pool surrounded by buffalo originally paired with Eliel's catenary curve design for the Jefferson Expansion Memorial.
